docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Nitin Pandit

Mentor

Connecting dots...

docs.title: About Me
docs.text:
Experienced Mentor with a demonstrated history in applied research in not-for-profits and private sector consulting, with a track record of strategic development of new initiatives leading to impact. Knowledgeable in Policy Design, Simulation Modeling and AI, Biodiversity and Climate Change, Civil and Water Resource Engineering, Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy. Strong professional with a PhD. in Public Policy from George Mason University.I am currently working to engage youth in the Shastri River Basin, Ratnagiri district, India.

View My Projects Download CV

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
• GIS & Remote Sensing

    • QGIS, ArcGIS Pro, Google Earth Engine
    • GDAL / OGR, GRASS GIS
    • Multispectral and SAR image analysis
    • Cloud Native Geospatial (COG, STAC, Zarr)

  • Programming

    • Python — GeoPandas, NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib
    • R — sf, terra, ggplot2
    • JavaScript — Leaflet, MapLibre GL
    • SQL, PostgreSQL + PostGIS

  • Machine Learning & GeoAI

    • Supervised classification — Random Forest, XGBoost
    • Deep learning for image segmentation — U-Net, SAM
    • scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow
    • Object detection in satellite imagery

  • Web Mapping & Data

    • Leaflet.js, Folium, MapLibre GL JS
    • Cloud storage — AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage
    • Data formats — GeoTIFF, GeoParquet, NetCDF
    • Streamlit for data-driven web apps

  • Data & Cloud

    • PostgreSQL + PostGIS
    • Cloud storage: AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage
    • Data formats: GeoJSON, GeoTIFF, NetCDF, Zarr, GeoParquet

  • Drone / UAV Data Processing

    • Mission planning and flight operations
    • Photogrammetry: Agisoft Metashape, OpenDroneMap
    • Point cloud processing: CloudCompare, PDAL

Email nitinspandit@gmail.com GitHub github.com/nitinspandit LinkedIn linkedin.com/in/nitinspandit

Download CV

docs.title: Independent Mentor — Self-Employed
docs.text:
January 2022 – Present | Washington, DC, USA

  • Developing a scalable low-impact development model in the Shastri River watershed in India, focused on Water-Energy-Biomass (WEB) initiatives for the rural poor.
  • Improving end-use efficiency, crop diversification, and resource conservation for asset creation on the demand side.
  • Cultivating partnerships with local colleges, researchers, and industry associations toward climate change mitigation and adaptation measures at the coast, estuary, and watershed scale.

docs.title: Director and CEO — ATREE (Ashoka Trust for Research in Ecology and the Environment)
docs.text:
January 2019 – December 2021 | India

  • Led development of a national program under the Alliance to Reverse Ecosystem Service Threats (AREST), presenting findings at COP26 in Glasgow and coordinating with India's National Mission on Biodiversity and Human Well-Being.
  • Launched two new centers — Center for Social and Environmental Impact and Center for Policy Design — mentoring their leadership to translate research into policy and market action.
  • Launched a Master's degree program in Conservation Practice to train the next generation of environmental leaders.

docs.title: Independent Consultant — Self-Employed
docs.text:
2017 – 2018 | Washington, DC, USA

  • Served as Strategic Advisor to the Chair of the ATREE Board.
  • Cultivated strategy for a new nanomaterials industry focused on restoring degraded lands.
  • Developed a data-driven tech startup concept for scaling the impact of energy efficiency programs.

docs.title: Director, Priority Initiatives — World Resources Institute
docs.text:
2016 – 2017 | Washington, DC, USA

  • Mobilized the Global Restoration Council (GRC), integrating research and communications across the Food, Forests, and Water programs toward policy scale-up of the Bonn Challenge (restoring 350M hectares by 2030).
  • Authored thought leadership pieces — op-eds, blogs, and presentations — on restoration's linkages with employment, natural infrastructure funding, and migration.
  • Proposed strategic entry points, including green and cool roofs, to integrate energy efficiency into the organization's global energy program.

docs.title: CEO — World Resources Institute India
docs.text:
2013 – 2016 | Mumbai, India

  • Developed and coordinated India resource management strategy with the global WRI network, including during the Paris climate change negotiations.
  • Cultivated innovative entry points such as Green Smart City-Regions and Distributed Energy for Utilities to lead program diversification.
  • Initiated a cross-sectoral landscape restoration program for climate change mitigation and adaptation, including strategic partnerships with organizations like NABARD.

docs.title: President — International Institute for Energy Conservation (IIEC)
docs.text:
2000 – 2013 | Washington, DC, USA (with offices in Bangkok, Manila, Pune, New Delhi)

  • Led a multinational team on award-winning market transformation programs for energy efficient equipment in China, India, the Philippines, and South Africa.
  • Guided development of monitoring and verification systems for a 100 million Euro KfW credit line for energy efficiency investments in Indian SMEs.
  • Built demand-side management (DSM) programs with private utilities including Reliance Energy, Tata Power, and BESCOM; piloted DSM projects in Vietnam, India, Laos, South Africa, and Sri Lanka.
  • Created the Mumbai Energy Alliance and developed green building brands and commercial energy codes in Thailand, Vietnam, and India.

docs.title: President — ENSYS, Inc.
docs.text:
1991 – 2000 | Vienna, VA, USA

  • Founded a boutique consultancy specializing in AI techniques, policy methods, and offset trading for renewable energy, water quality, and environmental problem-solving.
  • Developed a portfolio risk knowledge management system using AI for Enron's carbon dioxide emissions projects.
  • Created an AI-based expert system for modeling tradable coal-fired power plant emissions for the Asian Development Bank.
  • Developed a novel GIS-based nutrient offset trading system for point/non-point source pollution control in the Tar-Pamlico basin, North Carolina.

docs.title: Project Director — Roy F. Weston, Inc.
docs.text:
1986 – 1991 | Washington, DC, USA

  • Led a multidisciplinary applied R&D department developing intelligent products for geo-environmental problem solving for the U.S. EPA and other clients.
  • Developed CARTS (Computer-Aided Remedial Technologies Selector), an object-oriented AI system using non-monotonic reasoning implemented in Lisp and KEE™.
  • Built expert systems for dam gate operations in the Sierra Nevada and chemical risk advisory for emergency personnel.

docs.title: Senior Geotechnical Engineer — Woodward-Clyde Consultants, Eastern Group
docs.text:
1981 – 1984 | USA

  • Conducted geotechnical characterization, modeling, and design with field and lab interactions.
  • Piloted novel construction techniques including geo-grids for pavement reinforcement.
  • Managed applications including power plant fly ash disposal, ground-water modeling, and construction of offshore and terrestrial foundations.

docs.title: Geohydrology Engineer Trainee — Dubon Project Engineering Pvt. Ltd.
docs.text:
1978 – 1979 | Bombay, India

  • Worked on water resource systems engineering for irrigation, drainage, and crop development projects in East Africa.
  • Researched low-cost local biomass-based construction materials in India.

docs.title: Doctor of Philosophy in Public Policy
docs.text:
George Mason University, Institute of Public Policy | 1998

Major in Energy and Institutional Economics. Thesis: A Common Property Approach for Retail Energy Institutions for Distributed Generation.

docs.title: Master of Science in Civil & Environmental Engineering
docs.text:
Carnegie Mellon University, The Robotics Institute | 1986

Major in Artificial Intelligence. Thesis: An Expert System for Mining Machine Condition Monitoring.

docs.title: Master of Science in Civil Engineering
docs.text:
Lehigh University, Fritz Engineering Laboratory | 1980

Major in Geotechnical Engineering. Thesis: Geotechnical Properties of Shanghai Soft Silt.

docs.title: Bachelor of Engineering (Honors) in Civil Engineering
docs.text:
Birla Institute of Technology and Science, Pilani | 1979

Major in Water Resource Systems. Thesis: Water Resource Development Methodology for West African Watersheds.

  • U.S. Patent 6,529,890 B1 — Method of representing synoptic climatology information in a class-object-attribute hierarchy and an expert system for obtaining synoptic climatology information
  • Member: ACM, WEF, IEEE, APPA

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
A selection of my geospatial projects. Click any card to see the full write-up.

Slug Test Interpretation

Pandit and Miner (1986) proposed a translation method for interpreting slug test data to reduce inconsistencies in estimating initial excess head, as commonly found in the Black (1978) method. This approach addresses subjectivity in matching field data to type curves, advocating for numerical methods for more consistent hydraulic conductivity ((K)) estimation.

HP 9845B running Basic Submersible Pressure Transducers

View Project →

Sample Notebook

[YOUR PROJECT DESCRIPTION — one or two sentences: what you did, what data you used, and what you found or built.]

Python pandas Folium

View Project →

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Pandit and Miner (1986) proposed a Translation Method for interpreting slug test data to reduce inconsistencies in estimating initial excess head, as commonly encountered in the Black (1978) method. This approach addresses subjectivity in matching field data to type curves, advocating for numerical methods to achieve more consistent hydraulic conductivity ((K)) estimation.

Study Area: Woodward-Clyde Consultants (WCC) test sites Duration: September 1984 – April 1986 Role: Team lead Status: Completed

docs.title: Methods & Tools
docs.text:
Data Sources

  • Slug test field measurements from WCC test wells (WCC No. 4, WCC No. 5, WCC-EW, WCC-LI)
  • Cooper et al. (1967) type curve dataset used for validation

Processing Steps

  1. Collected field voltage records using submersible pressure transducers and strip chart recorders, converting output to excess head H(t) via transducer calibration
  2. Evaluated three methods for computing initial excess head H(0): theoretical computation, Regression Method, and the proposed Translation Method
  3. Applied a least-squares numerical curve-matching procedure to replace subjective visual matching to type curves
  4. Compared transmissivity (T) and hydraulic conductivity (K) estimates across all three H(0) methods
  5. Developed and tested an interactive BASIC software package on a Hewlett-Packard 9845B personal computer for consistent, automated slug test analysis

Tools Used

Tool Purpose HP 9845B Personal Computer Running the interactive BASIC software package BASIC Programming Language Implementing the numerical curve-matching and Translation Method Submersible Pressure Transducers Field data collection Least-Squares Error Minimization Objective type-curve matching

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The Translation Method produced transmissivity estimates in close agreement with the Regression Method (e.g., T = 1.4 vs 1.57 cm²/sec for WCC No. 4), confirming its accuracy
  • Automating slug test analysis via numerical curve-matching reduced analysis time by up to 50% compared to manual methods
  • The Translation Method is more direct and consistent than existing methods, minimizing subjective judgment in H(0) estimation
  • Reliable results require the steady response curve to begin within 10% of the time needed to dissipate 80% of the initial excess head
  • Storage coefficients implied by type-curve matching showed order-of-magnitude variation across data sets and should not be used alone to identify confined vs. unconfined conditions

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import folium
import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import folium In [ ]: Copied! 
# Placeholder time-series data
# Replace with: df = pd.read_csv("your_data.csv")
data = {
    "month": ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
              "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"],
    "metric_a": [120, 135, 148, 162, 175, 190, 185, 178, 160, 145, 130, 118],
    "metric_b": [ 80,  88,  95, 105, 112, 120, 118, 110, 100,  92,  85,  78],
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df.head()

df[["metric_a", "metric_b"]].describe()
df[["metric_a", "metric_b"]].describe() In [4]: Copied! 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))

ax.plot(df["month"], df["metric_a"], marker="o", label="Metric A", color="steelblue")
ax.plot(df["month"], df["metric_b"], marker="s", label="Metric B", color="coral")

ax.set_title("[YOUR CHART TITLE]", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("[Unit]")
ax.legend()
ax.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4)) ax.plot(df["month"], df["metric_a"], marker="o", label="Metric A", color="steelblue") ax.plot(df["month"], df["metric_b"], marker="s", label="Metric B", color="coral") ax.set_title("[YOUR CHART TITLE]", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold") ax.set_xlabel("Month") ax.set_ylabel("[Unit]") ax.legend() ax.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() In [7]: Copied! 
# Placeholder point locations
# Replace with your own lat/lon data, e.g. from a GeoDataFrame or CSV
sites = pd.DataFrame({
    "name":  ["Site A", "Site B", "Site C", "Site D", "Site E"],
    "lat":   [28.61,    28.65,    28.58,    28.70,    28.55],
    "lon":   [77.20,    77.23,    77.17,    77.28,    77.15],
    "value": [142,      168,      95,       210,       87],
})

# Centre the map on the mean location
m = folium.Map(
    location=[sites["lat"].mean(), sites["lon"].mean()],
    zoom_start=11,
    tiles="CartoDB positron",
)

# Add a CircleMarker for each site, scaled by value
for _, row in sites.iterrows():
    folium.CircleMarker(
        location=[row["lat"], row["lon"]],
        radius=row["value"] / 20,          # scale radius to value
        color="steelblue",
        fill=True,
        fill_opacity=0.7,
        tooltip=f"{row['name']}: {row['value']}",
        popup=folium.Popup(
            f"<b>{row['name']}</b><br>Value: {row['value']}",
            max_width=200,
        ),
    ).add_to(m)

m
